Question: What does 'MIL' stand for in the context of distantly supervised relation extraction?
Summary:
Title: A Sample-Based Training Method for Distantly Supervised Relation
  Extraction with Pre-Trained Transformers 
Summary: Multiple instance learning (MIL) has become the standard learning paradigm
for distantly supervised relation extraction (DSRE). However, due to relation
extraction being performed at bag level, MIL has significant hardware
requirements for training when coupled with large sentence encoders such as
deep transformer neural networks. In this paper, we propose a novel sampling
method for DSRE that relaxes these hardware requirements. In the proposed
method, we limit the number of sentences in a batch by randomly sampling
sentences from the bags in the batch. However, this comes at the cost of losing
valid sentences from bags. To alleviate the issues caused by random sampling,
we use an ensemble of trained models for prediction. We demonstrate the
effectiveness of our approach by using our proposed learning setting to
fine-tuning BERT on the widely NYT dataset. Our approach significantly
outperforms previous state-of-the-art methods in terms of AUC and P@N metrics.
llama3.2:1b: In the context of distantly supervised relation extraction, "MIL" stands for Multiple Instance Learning. It is a learning paradigm used to extract relations from unlabeled data, where multiple instances or samples are learned together to represent each labeled instance. This approach has been widely adopted in various applications, including relation extraction tasks.
llama3.2:3b: In the context of distantly supervised relation extraction, "MIL" stands for Multiple Instance Learning. It is a standard learning paradigm used for this task, but it has significant hardware requirements due to the need to process large sentence encoders like deep transformer neural networks.
phi3.5:3.8b: Multiple Instance Learning (MIL) is a standard learning paradigm used for distantly supervised Relation Extraction. It typically requires significant hardware resources when coupled with large sentence encoders like deep transformer neural networks, as mentioned in the first document about training methods that reduce these requirements through novel sampling approaches.
gemma2:2b: MIL stands for Multiple Instance Learning, which is a learning paradigm commonly used for distant supervised relation extraction tasks.
qwen2.5:3b: Multiple instance learning (MIL) is a standard learning paradigm used in the context of distantly supervised relation extraction (DSRE) to handle the bag-level relations efficiently, despite requiring significant hardware resources when coupled with large sentence encoders like deep transformer neural networks.

Question: What does the acronym 'TCBERT' represent?
Summary:
Title: TCBERT: A Technical Report for Chinese Topic Classification BERT 
Summary: Bidirectional Encoder Representations from Transformers or
BERT~\cite{devlin-etal-2019-bert} has been one of the base models for various
NLP tasks due to its remarkable performance. Variants customized for different
languages and tasks are proposed to further improve the performance. In this
work, we investigate supervised continued
pre-training~\cite{gururangan-etal-2020-dont} on BERT for Chinese topic
classification task. Specifically, we incorporate prompt-based learning and
contrastive learning into the pre-training. To adapt to the task of Chinese
topic classification, we collect around 2.1M Chinese data spanning various
topics. The pre-trained Chinese Topic Classification BERTs (TCBERTs) with
different parameter sizes are open-sourced at
\url{https://huggingface.co/IDEA-CCNL}.
llama3.2:1b: The acronym "TCBERT" represents Technical Chinese BERT, a technical report for Chinese topic classification by BERT developed by researchers. Specifically, it stands for Technical Chinese BERT for Topic Classification, which aims to improve upon previous models for Chinese topic classification tasks.
llama3.2:3b: The acronym 'TCBERT' represents Technical Report for Chinese Topic Classification BERT, a variant of the Bidirectional Encoder Representations from Transformers (BERT) model customized for Chinese topic classification tasks. This model was developed by incorporating prompt-based learning and contrastive learning into pre-training to improve performance on Chinese text classification tasks.
phi3.5:3.8b: Technical Chinese Topic Classification BERT (TCBERT) is a variant of BERT customized for the topic classification in Mandarin, incorporating prompt-based learning and contrastive learning into its pre-training. It was open-sourced to facilitate research on Chinese topics with improved performance compared to standard models without task adaptation features like TCBERT have shown.
gemma2:2b: TCBERT stands for "Chinese Topic BERT".  It is a pre-trained language model specifically adapted for Chinese topic classification tasks.
qwen2.5:3b: No Answer

Question: What is the 'Instruction Following Score' (IFS) designed to measure in language models?
Summary:
Title: Becoming self-instruct: introducing early stopping criteria for minimal
  instruct tuning 
Summary: In this paper, we introduce the Instruction Following Score (IFS), a metric
that detects language models' ability to follow instructions. The metric has a
dual purpose. First, IFS can be used to distinguish between base and instruct
models. We benchmark publicly available base and instruct models, and show that
the ratio of well formatted responses to partial and full sentences can be an
effective measure between those two model classes. Secondly, the metric can be
used as an early stopping criteria for instruct tuning. We compute IFS for
Supervised Fine-Tuning (SFT) of 7B and 13B LLaMA models, showing that models
learn to follow instructions relatively early in the training process, and the
further finetuning can result in changes in the underlying base model
semantics. As an example of semantics change we show the objectivity of model
predictions, as defined by an auxiliary metric ObjecQA. We show that in this
particular case, semantic changes are the steepest when the IFS tends to
plateau. We hope that decomposing instruct tuning into IFS and semantic factors
starts a new trend in better controllable instruct tuning and opens
possibilities for designing minimal instruct interfaces querying foundation
models.
llama3.2:1b: The "Instruction Following Score" (IFS) is a metric designed to measure a language model's ability to follow instructions, which can be used for instruction tuning and other applications. In this context, IFS aims to distinguish between base and instruct models by comparing the ratio of well-formatted responses to partial and full sentences in both classes.
llama3.2:3b: The "Instruction Following Score" (IFS) is a metric designed to measure a language model's ability to follow instructions. It assesses whether the model can generate well-formatted responses that adhere to grammatical rules, syntax, and semantics, distinguishing it from base models. IFS serves as an early stopping criteria for instruct tuning in language models.
phi3.5:3.8b: The Instruction Following Score (IFS) measures a language model's ability to follow instructions by comparing well-formatted responses to partial and full sentences, distinguishing between base and instruct tuned models. IFS can also be used as an early stopping criteria for instruct tuning.
gemma2:2b: The Instruction Following Score (IFS) measures a language model's ability to follow instructions. It distinguishes between base and instruct models, with well-formatted responses indicating a strong ability to follow instruction.
qwen2.5:3b: The Instruction Following Score (IFS) is a metric introduced in the paper that evaluates and distinguishes between base and instruction-following language models. Specifically, IFS measures the ratio of well-formatted responses from partial or full sentences compared to those that are not formatted correctly when following instructions. It serves as both a distinguishing factor between different model classes and an early stopping criterion for instruct tuning, guiding the optimization process in developing more aligned models with specific instruction prompts.

Question: According to article abs-2308.00319v2, what is a hard-label attack?
Summary:
Title: LimeAttack: Local Explainable Method for Textual Hard-Label Adversarial
  Attack 
Summary: Natural language processing models are vulnerable to adversarial examples.
Previous textual adversarial attacks adopt gradients or confidence scores to
calculate word importance ranking and generate adversarial examples. However,
this information is unavailable in the real world. Therefore, we focus on a
more realistic and challenging setting, named hard-label attack, in which the
attacker can only query the model and obtain a discrete prediction label.
Existing hard-label attack algorithms tend to initialize adversarial examples
by random substitution and then utilize complex heuristic algorithms to
optimize the adversarial perturbation. These methods require a lot of model
queries and the attack success rate is restricted by adversary initialization.
In this paper, we propose a novel hard-label attack algorithm named LimeAttack,
which leverages a local explainable method to approximate word importance
ranking, and then adopts beam search to find the optimal solution. Extensive
experiments show that LimeAttack achieves the better attacking performance
compared with existing hard-label attack under the same query budget. In
addition, we evaluate the effectiveness of LimeAttack on large language models,
and results indicate that adversarial examples remain a significant threat to
large language models. The adversarial examples crafted by LimeAttack are
highly transferable and effectively improve model robustness in adversarial
training.
llama3.2:1b: A hard-label attack in this context refers to an adversarial attack where the attacker can only query the model and obtain a discrete prediction label, rather than manipulating the input text to generate an adversarial example. This approach leverages a local explainable method to approximate word importance ranking and utilizes beam search to find the optimal solution, as described in the article "LimeAttack: Local Explainable Method for Textual Hard-Label Adversarial" by [Authors].
llama3.2:3b: A hard-label attack in the context of article abs-2308.00319v2 refers to an adversarial attack where the attacker can only query the model and obtain a discrete prediction label, unlike existing attacks that rely on gradients or confidence scores. This makes the attack more realistic and challenging.
phi3.5:3.8b: A hard-label attack in the context of textual adversarial attacks refers to an approach where the attacker can only query the model and receive discrete prediction labels without access to detailed explanations or confidence scores. This setting poses significant challenges for crafting effective adversarial examples, which necessitates complex heuristic algorithms that are typically initialized randomly before optimization with limited queries under a specified budget.
gemma2:2b: A hard-label attack leverages a more realistic setting where an attacker can query the model and receive a discrete prediction label, rather than relying on gradients or confidence scores for word importance ranking. This method focuses on generating adversarial examples directly from model predictions.
qwen2.5:3b: Hard-label attacks in this context refer to adversarial examples where the attacker can only query the model for discrete prediction labels and cannot use gradient or confidence scores to calculate word importance ranking. The paper introduces LimeAttack, a novel algorithm that uses local explainable methods to approximate word importance rankings and employs beam search to find optimal solutions, outperforming existing hard-label attack algorithms under the same query budget.

Question: What is the main goal of the 'Multi-User MultiWOZ' dataset?
Summary:
Title: Multi-User MultiWOZ: Task-Oriented Dialogues among Multiple Users 
Summary: While most task-oriented dialogues assume conversations between the agent and
one user at a time, dialogue systems are increasingly expected to communicate
with multiple users simultaneously who make decisions collaboratively. To
facilitate development of such systems, we release the Multi-User MultiWOZ
dataset: task-oriented dialogues among two users and one agent. To collect this
dataset, each user utterance from MultiWOZ 2.2 was replaced with a small chat
between two users that is semantically and pragmatically consistent with the
original user utterance, thus resulting in the same dialogue state and system
response. These dialogues reflect interesting dynamics of collaborative
decision-making in task-oriented scenarios, e.g., social chatter and
deliberation. Supported by this data, we propose the novel task of multi-user
contextual query rewriting: to rewrite a task-oriented chat between two users
as a concise task-oriented query that retains only task-relevant information
and that is directly consumable by the dialogue system. We demonstrate that in
multi-user dialogues, using predicted rewrites substantially improves dialogue
state tracking without modifying existing dialogue systems that are trained for
single-user dialogues. Further, this method surpasses training a medium-sized
model directly on multi-user dialogues and generalizes to unseen domains.
llama3.2:1b: The primary objective of the "Multi-User MultiWOZ" dataset, as described in the retrieved documents, appears to be to facilitate the development of dialogue systems that can communicate with multiple users simultaneously who make decisions collaboratively. The dataset consists of task-oriented dialogues among two users and one agent, where each user utterance from the original MultiWOZ 2.2 dataset is replaced with a small chat that maintains semantic and pragmatic consistency.
llama3.2:3b: The main goal of the 'Multi-User MultiWOZ' dataset is to facilitate the development of dialogue systems that can communicate with multiple users simultaneously, allowing them to collaborate on decision-making tasks.
phi3.5:3.8b: The primary objective of the "Multi-User MultiWOZ" dataset is to facilitate research in developing dialogue systems capable of managing conversations with multiple users simultaneously, focusing on collaborative decision-making. It aims at enabling such communication by rewriting multi-user interactions into concise task-oriented queries consumable for existing single-user system models through novel methods like multilingual contextual query rewriting and NLP techniques to track dialogue states more effectively in these complex scenarios.
gemma2:2b: The 'Multi-User MultiWOZ' dataset aims to facilitate the development of task-oriented dialogue systems that allow for communication between multiple users collaboratively.
qwen2.5:3b: The 'Multi-User MultiWOZ' dataset aims to facilitate development of dialogue systems capable of communicating with multiple users simultaneously who make decisions collaboratively, by providing task-oriented dialogues among two users and one agent. These dialogues reflect interesting dynamics such as social chatter and deliberation in collaborative decision-making scenarios.

Question: Summarize the primary purpose of the 'AdapterFusion' framework for transfer learning.
Summary:
Title: AdapterFusion: Non-Destructive Task Composition for Transfer Learning 
Summary: Sequential fine-tuning and multi-task learning are methods aiming to
incorporate knowledge from multiple tasks; however, they suffer from
catastrophic forgetting and difficulties in dataset balancing. To address these
shortcomings, we propose AdapterFusion, a new two stage learning algorithm that
leverages knowledge from multiple tasks. First, in the knowledge extraction
stage we learn task specific parameters called adapters, that encapsulate the
task-specific information. We then combine the adapters in a separate knowledge
composition step. We show that by separating the two stages, i.e., knowledge
extraction and knowledge composition, the classifier can effectively exploit
the representations learned from multiple tasks in a non-destructive manner. We
empirically evaluate AdapterFusion on 16 diverse NLU tasks, and find that it
effectively combines various types of knowledge at different layers of the
model. We show that our approach outperforms traditional strategies such as
full fine-tuning as well as multi-task learning. Our code and adapters are
available at AdapterHub.ml.
llama3.2:1b: The primary purpose of the 'AdapterFusion' framework is to address two key shortcomings in traditional transfer learning methods: catastrophic forgetting and dataset balancing. AdapterFusion separates task-specific knowledge into two stages, first extracting task-specific parameters (adapters) through a "knowledge extraction" stage, and then combining these adapters in a separate "knowledge composition" stage. This approach enables the classifier to effectively exploit representations learned from multiple tasks without suffering from catastrophic forgetting or difficulties in dataset balancing.
llama3.2:3b: The primary purpose of the 'AdapterFusion' framework is to address catastrophic forgetting and dataset balancing issues in sequential fine-tuning and multi-task learning by separating knowledge extraction and composition into two stages. This allows classifiers to effectively exploit representations learned from multiple tasks in a non-destructive manner, outperforming traditional strategies such as full fine-tuning and multi-task learning.
phi3.5:3.8b: The AdapterFusion framework aims to combine knowledge from multiple tasks in a two-stage process that minimizes catastrophic forgetting and enables effective non-destructive exploitation of representations learned across different datasets, outperforming traditional fine-tuning methods for transfer learning.
gemma2:2b: AdapterFusion is a two-stage learning algorithm designed to tackle the challenges of catastrophic forgetting and dataset imbalance in transfer learning methods. It first extracts task-specific information (adapters) during knowledge extraction, followed by combining them in a separate composition step to enable non-destructive exploitation of representations from multiple tasks. This approach outperforms traditional fine-tuning and multi-task learning in various NLP benchmarks.
qwen2.5:3b: The AdapterFusion framework aims to address the issues of catastrophic forgetting and difficulty in dataset balancing associated with sequential fine-tuning and multi-task learning. It does so by separating the knowledge extraction stage from the knowledge composition stage, where task-specific adapters are first learned encapsulating the task-specific information, and then these adapters are combined separately. This approach allows for an effective exploitation of representations learned from multiple tasks without damaging their integrity.

Question: What problem does the 'LimeAttack' algorithm aim to solve in the context of adversarial attacks?
Summary:
Title: LimeAttack: Local Explainable Method for Textual Hard-Label Adversarial
  Attack 
Summary: Natural language processing models are vulnerable to adversarial examples.
Previous textual adversarial attacks adopt gradients or confidence scores to
calculate word importance ranking and generate adversarial examples. However,
this information is unavailable in the real world. Therefore, we focus on a
more realistic and challenging setting, named hard-label attack, in which the
attacker can only query the model and obtain a discrete prediction label.
Existing hard-label attack algorithms tend to initialize adversarial examples
by random substitution and then utilize complex heuristic algorithms to
optimize the adversarial perturbation. These methods require a lot of model
queries and the attack success rate is restricted by adversary initialization.
In this paper, we propose a novel hard-label attack algorithm named LimeAttack,
which leverages a local explainable method to approximate word importance
ranking, and then adopts beam search to find the optimal solution. Extensive
experiments show that LimeAttack achieves the better attacking performance
compared with existing hard-label attack under the same query budget. In
addition, we evaluate the effectiveness of LimeAttack on large language models,
and results indicate that adversarial examples remain a significant threat to
large language models. The adversarial examples crafted by LimeAttack are
highly transferable and effectively improve model robustness in adversarial
training.
llama3.2:1b: The "LimeAttack" algorithm aims to address a specific limitation in natural language processing (NLP) models, specifically their vulnerability to adversarial attacks. In traditional hard-label attack methods, attackers can query the model with a discrete prediction label and obtain a result. However, this approach is not realistic as it requires an adversary to have access to the model's parameters or a large number of queries. To overcome this limitation, "LimeAttack" leverages a local explainable method to approximate word importance ranking in the presence of adversarial examples.
llama3.2:3b: The 'LimeAttack' algorithm aims to solve the problem of generating adversarial examples that can bypass real-world quality gates, focusing on a more realistic and challenging setting known as the "hard-label attack." It proposes a novel method leveraging local explainable methods to approximate word importance ranking and then employs beam search to find the optimal solution.
phi3.5:3.8b: The LimeAttack algorithm addresses challenges faced by existing hard-label attack methods, which are limited due to their heavy reliance on initial random perturbations and complex optimization for generating adversarial examples. By using a local explainable method from 'Lime' (Local Interpretable Model-agnostic Explanations) combined with beam search, LimeAttack approximates word importance ranking more effectively under query budget constraints while acholding better attack performance on natural language processing models compared to its predecessors in the same context.
gemma2:2b: The LimeAttack algorithm aims to address the challenge of adversarial attacks by providing a more realistic and effective method than existing techniques. It leverages a local explainable method to approximate word importance ranking and then utilizes beam search for optimal solution finding, leading to better attacking performance compared to existing methods.
qwen2.5:3b: The 'LimeAttack' algorithm aims to address the challenge of performing effective hard-label adversarial text attacks, where attackers can only query the model for a discrete prediction label. It solves this by leveraging a local explainable method to approximate word importance ranking and using beam search to find the optimal solution, thereby improving attack performance compared to existing methods that require more model queries and suffer from restricted success rates due to adversary initialization.

Question: What is the main objective of the Evol-Instruct method as applied in the WizardCoder paper?
Summary:
Title: WizardCoder: Empowering Code Large Language Models with Evol-Instruct 
Summary: Code Large Language Models (Code LLMs), such as StarCoder, have demonstrated
exceptional performance in code-related tasks. However, most existing models
are solely pre-trained on extensive raw code data without instruction
fine-tuning. In this paper, we introduce WizardCoder, which empowers Code LLMs
with complex instruction fine-tuning, by adapting the Evol-Instruct method to
the domain of code. Through comprehensive experiments on four prominent code
generation benchmarks, namely HumanEval, HumanEval+, MBPP, and DS-1000, we
unveil the exceptional capabilities of our model. It surpasses all other
open-source Code LLMs by a substantial margin. Moreover, our model even
outperforms the largest closed LLMs, Anthropic's Claude and Google's Bard, on
HumanEval and HumanEval+. Our code, model weights, and data are public at
https://github.com/nlpxucan/WizardLM
llama3.2:1b: The main objective of the Evol-Instruct method, as applied in the WizardCoder paper, appears to be enhancing the performance of large language models (LLMs) by fine-tuning them on complex instruction data. Specifically, the Evol-Instruct method is designed to adapt existing LLMs to incorporate a wide range of instructions and tasks, effectively "fine-tuning" their representations to better suit specific applications or domains.
llama3.2:3b: The Evol-Instruct method, as applied in the WizardCoder paper, aims to empower Code Large Language Models (Code LLMs) with complex instruction fine-tuning. The primary goal of this approach is to adapt Code LLMs to receive and execute specific instructions, thereby enhancing their performance in code-related tasks.
phi3.5:3.8b: The Evol-Instruct method, when adapted through WizardCoder, aims to empower Code Large Language Models with complex instruction fine-0.5fine tuning for enhanced code generation capabilities and outperformance over existing models on prominent benchmarks.
gemma2:2b: WizardCoder applies Evol-Instruct to empower Code LLMs with complex instruction fine-tuning. This method helps the model learn to follow human-provided instructions for generating code, leading to enhanced capabilities compared to pre-trained models solely based on raw code data.
qwen2.5:3b: The main objective of the Evol-Instruct method, as applied in the WizardCoder paper, is to empower Code Large Language Models with complex instruction fine-tuning. This adaptation of the Evol-Instruct method enables WizardCoder to achieve exceptional performance on various code generation benchmarks, surpassing other open-source models and even outperforming large closed LLMs like Anthropic's Claude and Google's Bard.

Question: Compare the approaches to data augmentation for low-resource scenarios in 'TreeSwap' (abs-2311.02355v1) and 'Adversarial Word Dilution' (abs-2305.09287v2).
Summary:
Title: TreeSwap: Data Augmentation for Machine Translation via Dependency
  Subtree Swapping 
Summary: Data augmentation methods for neural machine translation are particularly
useful when limited amount of training data is available, which is often the
case when dealing with low-resource languages. We introduce a novel
augmentation method, which generates new sentences by swapping objects and
subjects across bisentences. This is performed simultaneously based on the
dependency parse trees of the source and target sentences. We name this method
TreeSwap. Our results show that TreeSwap achieves consistent improvements over
baseline models in 4 language pairs in both directions on resource-constrained
datasets. We also explore domain-specific corpora, but find that our method
does not make significant improvements on law, medical and IT data. We report
the scores of similar augmentation methods and find that TreeSwap performs
comparably. We also analyze the generated sentences qualitatively and find that
the augmentation produces a correct translation in most cases. Our code is
available on Github. 

Title: Adversarial Word Dilution as Text Data Augmentation in Low-Resource
  Regime 
Summary: Data augmentation is widely used in text classification, especially in the
low-resource regime where a few examples for each class are available during
training. Despite the success, generating data augmentations as hard positive
examples that may increase their effectiveness is under-explored. This paper
proposes an Adversarial Word Dilution (AWD) method that can generate hard
positive examples as text data augmentations to train the low-resource text
classification model efficiently. Our idea of augmenting the text data is to
dilute the embedding of strong positive words by weighted mixing with
unknown-word embedding, making the augmented inputs hard to be recognized as
positive by the classification model. We adversarially learn the dilution
weights through a constrained min-max optimization process with the guidance of
the labels. Empirical studies on three benchmark datasets show that AWD can
generate more effective data augmentations and outperform the state-of-the-art
text data augmentation methods. The additional analysis demonstrates that the
data augmentations generated by AWD are interpretable and can flexibly extend
to new examples without further training.
llama3.2:1b:
Based on the retrieved documents, both "TreeSwap" (abs-2311.02355v1) and "Adversarial Word Dilution" (abs-2305.09287v2) propose data augmentation methods for low-resource scenarios in text classification tasks.

"TreeSwap", a novel method introduced by [Author] in abs-2311.02355v1, generates new sentences by swapping objects and subjects across bisentences based on the dependency parse trees of source and target sentences. This approach is controllable and has shown improvements over baseline models in 4 language pairs.

"Adversarial Word Dilution", as proposed in abs-2305.09287v2, utilizes adversarial learning to generate hard positive examples by diluting the embedding of strong positive words with unknown-word embeddings. The method leverages a constrained min-max optimization process for optimal results and has demonstrated outperformance over state-of-the-art methods.

While both approaches address low-resource scenarios in text classification tasks, they differ in their methodologies. "TreeSwap" relies on dependency parse trees to generate new sentences, whereas "Adversarial Word Dilution" uses adversarial learning to augment word embeddings directly. Additionally, "TreeSwap" focuses on aspect term extraction and compositional generalization, whereas "Adversarial Word Dilution" primarily targets text classification tasks.
llama3.2:3b: Both TreeSwap (abs-2311.02355v1) and Adversarial Word Dilution (abs-2305.09287v2) propose novel approaches to data augmentation for low-resource scenarios in machine translation and text classification, respectively. TreeSwap generates new sentences by swapping objects and subjects across bisentences based on dependency parse trees, while Adversarial Word Dilution dilutes the embedding of strong positive words to create hard positive examples for training. Both methods demonstrate effectiveness and outperform state-of-the-art approaches, with TreeSwap showing consistent improvements in resource-constrained datasets and Adversarial Word Dilution generating more effective data augmentations.
phi3.5:3.8b: Both TreeSwap and Adversarial Word Dilution aim to enhance data augmentation in low-resource scenarios: TreeSwap generates new sentences by swapping objects/subjects across bisentences based on dependency parse trees, while AWD creates hard positive examples for text classification through diluting strong word embeddings. Empirical studies show that both methods improve performance compared to baselines; however, specific results vary depending on the dataset and task context.
gemma2:2b: Both "TreeSwap" and "Adversarial Word Dilution" aim to enhance data augmentation for low-resource scenarios. TreeSwap employs dependency tree swapping, generating new sentences by manipulating subject and object positions between bisentences. AWD utilizes adversarial learning to dilute strong positive words in text embeddings through weighted mixing with unknown-word embeddings, creating harder inputs for the model to recognize as positive examples.  TreeSwap has shown consistent improvements over baseline models across various language pairs, while AWD demonstrates effectiveness through empirical studies on benchmark datasets and achieves interpretability with its generated data.
qwen2.5:3b: 'TreeSwap' proposes a method to generate new sentences by swapping objects and subjects across bisentences, leveraging dependency parse trees of the source and target sentences. In contrast, 'Adversarial Word Dilution' focuses on diluting the embedding of strong positive words through weighted mixing with unknown-word embeddings, aiming to create harder positive examples for training low-resource text classification models efficiently. Both methods address the challenge of data sparsity in low-resource scenarios but employ different techniques: TreeSwap uses syntactic information and structural manipulation, while AWD employs a semantic-based approach to dilute word embeddings.

Question: How do the evaluation goals of the 'Sem-nCG' metric (abs-2308.02270v2) differ from the 'FEQA' metric (abs-2005.03754v1) for abstractive summarization?
Summary:
Title: Redundancy Aware Multi-Reference Based Gainwise Evaluation of Extractive
  Summarization 
Summary: The ROUGE metric is commonly used to evaluate extractive summarization task,
but it has been criticized for its lack of semantic awareness and its ignorance
about the ranking quality of the extractive summarizer. Previous research has
introduced a gain-based automated metric called Sem-nCG that addresses these
issues, as it is both rank and semantic aware. However, it does not consider
the amount of redundancy present in a model summary and currently does not
support evaluation with multiple reference summaries. It is essential to have a
model summary that balances importance and diversity, but finding a metric that
captures both of these aspects is challenging. In this paper, we propose a
redundancy-aware Sem-nCG metric and demonstrate how the revised Sem-nCG metric
can be used to evaluate model summaries against multiple references as well
which was missing in previous research. Experimental results demonstrate that
the revised Sem-nCG metric has a stronger correlation with human judgments
compared to the previous Sem-nCG metric and traditional ROUGE and BERTScore
metric for both single and multiple reference scenarios. 

Title: FEQA: A Question Answering Evaluation Framework for Faithfulness
  Assessment in Abstractive Summarization 
Summary: Neural abstractive summarization models are prone to generate content
inconsistent with the source document, i.e. unfaithful. Existing automatic
metrics do not capture such mistakes effectively. We tackle the problem of
evaluating faithfulness of a generated summary given its source document. We
first collected human annotations of faithfulness for outputs from numerous
models on two datasets. We find that current models exhibit a trade-off between
abstractiveness and faithfulness: outputs with less word overlap with the
source document are more likely to be unfaithful. Next, we propose an automatic
question answering (QA) based metric for faithfulness, FEQA, which leverages
recent advances in reading comprehension. Given question-answer pairs generated
from the summary, a QA model extracts answers from the document; non-matched
answers indicate unfaithful information in the summary. Among metrics based on
word overlap, embedding similarity, and learned language understanding models,
our QA-based metric has significantly higher correlation with human
faithfulness scores, especially on highly abstractive summaries.
llama3.2:1b:
The 'Sem-nCG' metric and the 'FEQA' metric are both evaluation tools used for abstractive summarization, but they have distinct goals:

The 'Sem-nCG' metric focuses on semantic awareness and ranking quality of extractive summarizers, aiming to evaluate model summaries that balance importance and diversity. It considers both the amount of redundancy present in a summary and its rank against multiple reference summaries.

On the other hand, the 'FEQA' metric is designed for faithfulness evaluation in abstractive summarization, focusing on whether the generated summary accurately represents the source document's content. It assesses models based on their ability to extract relevant information from the source material.

While both metrics aim to evaluate the quality of summaries, they have different priorities: Sem-nCG prioritizes semantic awareness and ranking, whereas FEQA prioritizes faithfulness.
llama3.2:3b: The Sem-nCG and FEQA metrics have distinct evaluation goals for abstractive summarization. While Sem-nCG focuses on semantic awareness and ranking quality, FEQA aims to assess the faithfulness of generated summaries by evaluating their consistency with the source document through a question-answering approach. This means that FEQA measures how accurately the summary reflects the original content, whereas Sem-nCG evaluates the semantic relevance and ranking of the summary without considering its faithfulness or consistency.
phi3.5:3.8b: The Sem-nCG metric focuses on evaluating extractive summaries with a balance of importance and diversity, including redundancy awareness; it also assesses the ranking quality against multiple references. Conversely, FEQA (abs-2005.03754v1) is designed to evaluate faithfulness in abstractive summarization by identifying unfaithful information through question answering mechanisms extracted from human annotations of summary outputs and source documents' alignment; it does not explicitly consider redundancy or ranking quality within its framework.
gemma2:2b: The Sem-nCG metric evaluates the faithfulness of a generated summary by comparing its answers to questions about the source document, while the FEQA metric focuses on evaluating the faithfulness of a generated summary compared to an original text through question answering pairs.
qwen2.5:3b:

Question: Contrast the methods for fine-tuning language models in 'STT: Soft Template Tuning' (abs-2207.08408v1) and the analysis of fine-tuning brittleness in 'Fine-Tuning Pretrained Language Models' (abs-2002.06305v1).
Summary:
Title: STT: Soft Template Tuning for Few-Shot Adaptation 
Summary: Prompt tuning has been an extremely effective tool to adapt a pre-trained
model to downstream tasks. However, standard prompt-based methods mainly
consider the case of sufficient data of downstream tasks. It is still unclear
whether the advantage can be transferred to the few-shot regime, where only
limited data are available for each downstream task. Although some works have
demonstrated the potential of prompt-tuning under the few-shot setting, the
main stream methods via searching discrete prompts or tuning soft prompts with
limited data are still very challenging. Through extensive empirical studies,
we find that there is still a gap between prompt tuning and fully fine-tuning
for few-shot learning. To bridge the gap, we propose a new prompt-tuning
framework, called Soft Template Tuning (STT). STT combines manual and auto
prompts, and treats downstream classification tasks as a masked language
modeling task. Comprehensive evaluation on different settings suggests STT can
close the gap between fine-tuning and prompt-based methods without introducing
additional parameters. Significantly, it can even outperform the time- and
resource-consuming fine-tuning method on sentiment classification tasks. 

Title: Fine-Tuning Pretrained Language Models: Weight Initializations, Data
  Orders, and Early Stopping 
Summary: Fine-tuning pretrained contextual word embedding models to supervised
downstream tasks has become commonplace in natural language processing. This
process, however, is often brittle: even with the same hyperparameter values,
distinct random seeds can lead to substantially different results. To better
understand this phenomenon, we experiment with four datasets from the GLUE
benchmark, fine-tuning BERT hundreds of times on each while varying only the
random seeds. We find substantial performance increases compared to previously
reported results, and we quantify how the performance of the best-found model
varies as a function of the number of fine-tuning trials. Further, we examine
two factors influenced by the choice of random seed: weight initialization and
training data order. We find that both contribute comparably to the variance of
out-of-sample performance, and that some weight initializations perform well
across all tasks explored. On small datasets, we observe that many fine-tuning
trials diverge part of the way through training, and we offer best practices
for practitioners to stop training less promising runs early. We publicly
release all of our experimental data, including training and validation scores
for 2,100 trials, to encourage further analysis of training dynamics during
fine-tuning.

Unlike the traditional fine-tuning method, which relies solely on data augmentation, STT integrates both explicit and implicit instructions to improve model adaptability. This approach has shown promising results in fine-tuning language models, outperforming traditional methods like fine-tuning on sentiment classification tasks.

While the latter proposes a data-driven approach using soft templates, the former highlights the importance of understanding fine-tuning brittleness through experiments varying random seeds, weight initializations, and training data orders. The findings in abs-2002.06305v1 demonstrate that even small variations in these factors can lead to significant differences in performance, underscoring the need for robust approaches to mitigate fine-tuning brittleness.

Conversely, "Fine-Tuning Pretrained Language Models" (abs-2002.06305v1) reveals that performance can vary significantly with different random seeds during the same hyperparameter settings and suggests using early stopping to manage brittleness; it also quantifies how best practices for fine-tuning should consider weight initialization, data order, as well as introducing variance in model performance based on trial numbers.

The paper explores different factors influencing variance, including weight initialization and data order, highlighting their impact on model performance during training.

Question: How does the approach to multi-task learning in 'AdapterFusion' (abs-2005.00247v3) differ from the approach to multi-domain text classification in 'MRAN' (abs-2102.00467v1)?
Summary:
Title: AdapterFusion: Non-Destructive Task Composition for Transfer Learning 
Summary: Sequential fine-tuning and multi-task learning are methods aiming to
incorporate knowledge from multiple tasks; however, they suffer from
catastrophic forgetting and difficulties in dataset balancing. To address these
shortcomings, we propose AdapterFusion, a new two stage learning algorithm that
leverages knowledge from multiple tasks. First, in the knowledge extraction
stage we learn task specific parameters called adapters, that encapsulate the
task-specific information. We then combine the adapters in a separate knowledge
composition step. We show that by separating the two stages, i.e., knowledge
extraction and knowledge composition, the classifier can effectively exploit
the representations learned from multiple tasks in a non-destructive manner. We
empirically evaluate AdapterFusion on 16 diverse NLU tasks, and find that it
effectively combines various types of knowledge at different layers of the
model. We show that our approach outperforms traditional strategies such as
full fine-tuning as well as multi-task learning. Our code and adapters are
available at AdapterHub.ml. 

Title: Mixup Regularized Adversarial Networks for Multi-Domain Text
  Classification 
Summary: Using the shared-private paradigm and adversarial training has significantly
improved the performances of multi-domain text classification (MDTC) models.
However, there are two issues for the existing methods. First, instances from
the multiple domains are not sufficient for domain-invariant feature
extraction. Second, aligning on the marginal distributions may lead to fatal
mismatching. In this paper, we propose a mixup regularized adversarial network
(MRAN) to address these two issues. More specifically, the domain and category
mixup regularizations are introduced to enrich the intrinsic features in the
shared latent space and enforce consistent predictions in-between training
instances such that the learned features can be more domain-invariant and
discriminative. We conduct experiments on two benchmarks: The Amazon review
dataset and the FDU-MTL dataset. Our approach on these two datasets yields
average accuracies of 87.64\% and 89.0\% respectively, outperforming all
relevant baselines.
llama3.2:1b:
The approach to multi-task learning in AdapterFusion differs from that of Multi-Domain Text Classification (MDTC) in MRAN by separating the knowledge extraction stage into two distinct steps: knowledge extraction and knowledge composition. In AdapterFusion, adapters are learned separately for each task, while in MDTC, a single model is trained to perform multiple tasks simultaneously.

In AdapterFusion, task-specific parameters called adapters are learned to encapsulate the unique information of each task. These adapters are then combined during the knowledge composition step to leverage knowledge from multiple tasks. In contrast, in MRAN, all instances are mixed together, and features extracted from the shared-private paradigm and adversarial training aim to improve domain-invariant feature extraction.

While both approaches use adversarial training to enhance feature representation, AdapterFusion takes a more modular approach by separating knowledge extraction and composition stages, whereas MDTC uses a single model that learns across multiple tasks simultaneously.

Both methods aim to improve task performance through transfer learning but utilize different mechanisms: AdapterFusion uses adapters encapsulating specific information from multiple tasks; MRAN focuses on enhancing consistency and domain invariance by mixing up instances across domains during pre-training.

In contrast, the Mixup Regularized Adversarial Networks (MRAN) approach in 'Mixup Regularized Adversarial Networks for Multi-Domain Text Classification' combines a multi-class classifier with multiple binary classifiers trained together using adversarial training and mixup regularization to address domain-invariant feature extraction issues across multiple domains.

Question: Could the 'LimeAttack' method (abs-2308.00319v2) be used to test the robustness of the 'WizardCoder' model (abs-2306.08568v1) against hard-label attacks on code generation tasks?
Summary:
Title: LimeAttack: Local Explainable Method for Textual Hard-Label Adversarial
  Attack 
Summary: Natural language processing models are vulnerable to adversarial examples.
Previous textual adversarial attacks adopt gradients or confidence scores to
calculate word importance ranking and generate adversarial examples. However,
this information is unavailable in the real world. Therefore, we focus on a
more realistic and challenging setting, named hard-label attack, in which the
attacker can only query the model and obtain a discrete prediction label.
Existing hard-label attack algorithms tend to initialize adversarial examples
by random substitution and then utilize complex heuristic algorithms to
optimize the adversarial perturbation. These methods require a lot of model
queries and the attack success rate is restricted by adversary initialization.
In this paper, we propose a novel hard-label attack algorithm named LimeAttack,
which leverages a local explainable method to approximate word importance
ranking, and then adopts beam search to find the optimal solution. Extensive
experiments show that LimeAttack achieves the better attacking performance
compared with existing hard-label attack under the same query budget. In
addition, we evaluate the effectiveness of LimeAttack on large language models,
and results indicate that adversarial examples remain a significant threat to
large language models. The adversarial examples crafted by LimeAttack are
highly transferable and effectively improve model robustness in adversarial
training. 

Title: WizardCoder: Empowering Code Large Language Models with Evol-Instruct 
Summary: Code Large Language Models (Code LLMs), such as StarCoder, have demonstrated
exceptional performance in code-related tasks. However, most existing models
are solely pre-trained on extensive raw code data without instruction
fine-tuning. In this paper, we introduce WizardCoder, which empowers Code LLMs
with complex instruction fine-tuning, by adapting the Evol-Instruct method to
the domain of code. Through comprehensive experiments on four prominent code
generation benchmarks, namely HumanEval, HumanEval+, MBPP, and DS-1000, we
unveil the exceptional capabilities of our model. It surpasses all other
open-source Code LLMs by a substantial margin. Moreover, our model even
outperforms the largest closed LLMs, Anthropic's Claude and Google's Bard, on
HumanEval and HumanEval+. Our code, model weights, and data are public at
https://github.com/nlpxucan/WizardLM

Question: How might the 'generator-validator consistency' framework from abs-2310.01846v1 be used to evaluate the faithfulness of summaries generated by models trained for structure-controllable generation on the 'MReD' dataset (abs-2110.07474v6)?
Summary:
Title: Benchmarking and Improving Generator-Validator Consistency of Language
  Models 
Summary: As of September 2023, ChatGPT correctly answers "what is 7+8" with 15, but
when asked "7+8=15, True or False" it responds with "False". This inconsistency
between generating and validating an answer is prevalent in language models
(LMs) and erodes trust. In this paper, we propose a framework for measuring the
consistency between generation and validation (which we call
generator-validator consistency, or GV-consistency), finding that even GPT-4, a
state-of-the-art LM, is GV-consistent only 76% of the time. To improve the
consistency of LMs, we propose to finetune on the filtered generator and
validator responses that are GV-consistent, and call this approach consistency
fine-tuning. We find that this approach improves GV-consistency of Alpaca-30B
from 60% to 93%, and the improvement extrapolates to unseen tasks and domains
(e.g., GV-consistency for positive style transfers extrapolates to unseen
styles like humor). In addition to improving consistency, consistency
fine-tuning improves both generator quality and validator accuracy without
using any labeled data. Evaluated across 6 tasks, including math questions,
knowledge-intensive QA, and instruction following, our method improves the
generator quality by 16% and the validator accuracy by 6.3% across all tasks. 

Title: MReD: A Meta-Review Dataset for Structure-Controllable Text Generation 
Summary: When directly using existing text generation datasets for controllable
generation, we are facing the problem of not having the domain knowledge and
thus the aspects that could be controlled are limited. A typical example is
when using CNN/Daily Mail dataset for controllable text summarization, there is
no guided information on the emphasis of summary sentences. A more useful text
generator should leverage both the input text and the control signal to guide
the generation, which can only be built with a deep understanding of the domain
knowledge. Motivated by this vision, our paper introduces a new text generation
dataset, named MReD. Our new dataset consists of 7,089 meta-reviews and all its
45k meta-review sentences are manually annotated with one of the 9 carefully
defined categories, including abstract, strength, decision, etc. We present
experimental results on start-of-the-art summarization models, and propose
methods for structure-controlled generation with both extractive and
abstractive models using our annotated data. By exploring various settings and
analyzing the model behavior with respect to the control signal, we demonstrate
the challenges of our proposed task and the values of our dataset MReD.
Meanwhile, MReD also allows us to have a better understanding of the
meta-review domain.

Question: Can the 'TreeSwap' data augmentation method (abs-2311.02355v1) be applied to the training data for the semantic parsing tasks in abs-2111.05013v1 to improve their compositional generalization?
Summary:
Title: TreeSwap: Data Augmentation for Machine Translation via Dependency
  Subtree Swapping 
Summary: Data augmentation methods for neural machine translation are particularly
useful when limited amount of training data is available, which is often the
case when dealing with low-resource languages. We introduce a novel
augmentation method, which generates new sentences by swapping objects and
subjects across bisentences. This is performed simultaneously based on the
dependency parse trees of the source and target sentences. We name this method
TreeSwap. Our results show that TreeSwap achieves consistent improvements over
baseline models in 4 language pairs in both directions on resource-constrained
datasets. We also explore domain-specific corpora, but find that our method
does not make significant improvements on law, medical and IT data. We report
the scores of similar augmentation methods and find that TreeSwap performs
comparably. We also analyze the generated sentences qualitatively and find that
the augmentation produces a correct translation in most cases. Our code is
available on Github. 

Title: Learning to Generalize Compositionally by Transferring Across Semantic
  Parsing Tasks 
Summary: Neural network models often generalize poorly to mismatched domains or
distributions. In NLP, this issue arises in particular when models are expected
to generalize compositionally, that is, to novel combinations of familiar words
and constructions. We investigate learning representations that facilitate
transfer learning from one compositional task to another: the representation
and the task-specific layers of the models are strategically trained
differently on a pre-finetuning task such that they generalize well on
mismatched splits that require compositionality. We apply this method to
semantic parsing, using three very different datasets, COGS, GeoQuery and SCAN,
used alternately as the pre-finetuning and target task. Our method
significantly improves compositional generalization over baselines on the test
set of the target task, which is held out during fine-tuning. Ablation studies
characterize the utility of the major steps in the proposed algorithm and
support our hypothesis.

Compositional generalization refers to a model's ability to perform well on unseen input data, particularly when dealing with out-of-distribution examples or novel situations not seen during training. Given that "TreeSwap" primarily targets generating new sentences based on dependency parse trees, it is plausible that this method could be adapted for improving compositional generalization in semantic parsing tasks.

By applying the "TreeSwap" method to the training data of abs-2111.05013v1, researchers might observe improvements in model performance on unseen test sets or novel semantic contexts, indicating enhanced compositional generalization capabilities. However, further experimentation and analysis are necessary to confirm whether this approach can yield significant gains over existing methods for improving semantic parsing.

In summary, while the direct applicability of "TreeSwap" to semantically parsing tasks is uncertain without explicit demonstration in abs-2111.05013v1, its potential for improving compositional generalization makes it an area worth exploring.

Question: Considering the 'Multi-User MultiWOZ' dataset (abs-2310.20479v1), how could the 'DialoKG' framework (abs-2204.09149v1) be adapted to incorporate knowledge graph reasoning into a collaborative, multi-user dialogue setting?
Summary:
Title: Multi-User MultiWOZ: Task-Oriented Dialogues among Multiple Users 
Summary: While most task-oriented dialogues assume conversations between the agent and
one user at a time, dialogue systems are increasingly expected to communicate
with multiple users simultaneously who make decisions collaboratively. To
facilitate development of such systems, we release the Multi-User MultiWOZ
dataset: task-oriented dialogues among two users and one agent. To collect this
dataset, each user utterance from MultiWOZ 2.2 was replaced with a small chat
between two users that is semantically and pragmatically consistent with the
original user utterance, thus resulting in the same dialogue state and system
response. These dialogues reflect interesting dynamics of collaborative
decision-making in task-oriented scenarios, e.g., social chatter and
deliberation. Supported by this data, we propose the novel task of multi-user
contextual query rewriting: to rewrite a task-oriented chat between two users
as a concise task-oriented query that retains only task-relevant information
and that is directly consumable by the dialogue system. We demonstrate that in
multi-user dialogues, using predicted rewrites substantially improves dialogue
state tracking without modifying existing dialogue systems that are trained for
single-user dialogues. Further, this method surpasses training a medium-sized
model directly on multi-user dialogues and generalizes to unseen domains. 

Title: DialoKG: Knowledge-Structure Aware Task-Oriented Dialogue Generation 
Summary: Task-oriented dialogue generation is challenging since the underlying
knowledge is often dynamic and effectively incorporating knowledge into the
learning process is hard. It is particularly challenging to generate both
human-like and informative responses in this setting. Recent research primarily
focused on various knowledge distillation methods where the underlying
relationship between the facts in a knowledge base is not effectively captured.
In this paper, we go one step further and demonstrate how the structural
information of a knowledge graph can improve the system's inference
capabilities. Specifically, we propose DialoKG, a novel task-oriented dialogue
system that effectively incorporates knowledge into a language model. Our
proposed system views relational knowledge as a knowledge graph and introduces
(1) a structure-aware knowledge embedding technique, and (2) a knowledge
graph-weighted attention masking strategy to facilitate the system selecting
relevant information during the dialogue generation. An empirical evaluation
demonstrates the effectiveness of DialoKG over state-of-the-art methods on
several standard benchmark datasets.

3. Multi-user context: Consider incorporating multi-user context information into the dialogue generation process, such as shared goals or preferences between users.

By adapting DialoKG in this way, it may be possible to create a more effective collaborative, multi-user dialogue system that takes into account external knowledge and improves emotional understanding through multiple user interactions.

Question: What was the exact number of human evaluators used to assess the Sem-nCG metric in article abs-2308.02270v2?
Summary:
Title: Redundancy Aware Multi-Reference Based Gainwise Evaluation of Extractive
  Summarization 
Summary: The ROUGE metric is commonly used to evaluate extractive summarization task,
but it has been criticized for its lack of semantic awareness and its ignorance
about the ranking quality of the extractive summarizer. Previous research has
introduced a gain-based automated metric called Sem-nCG that addresses these
issues, as it is both rank and semantic aware. However, it does not consider
the amount of redundancy present in a model summary and currently does not
support evaluation with multiple reference summaries. It is essential to have a
model summary that balances importance and diversity, but finding a metric that
captures both of these aspects is challenging. In this paper, we propose a
redundancy-aware Sem-nCG metric and demonstrate how the revised Sem-nCG metric
can be used to evaluate model summaries against multiple references as well
which was missing in previous research. Experimental results demonstrate that
the revised Sem-nCG metric has a stronger correlation with human judgments
compared to the previous Sem-nCG metric and traditional ROUGE and BERTScore
metric for both single and multiple reference scenarios.

Question: In the 'LimeAttack' paper (abs-2308.00319v2), what was the average query count required to successfully attack the IMDB dataset?
Summary:
Title: LimeAttack: Local Explainable Method for Textual Hard-Label Adversarial
  Attack 
Summary: Natural language processing models are vulnerable to adversarial examples.
Previous textual adversarial attacks adopt gradients or confidence scores to
calculate word importance ranking and generate adversarial examples. However,
this information is unavailable in the real world. Therefore, we focus on a
more realistic and challenging setting, named hard-label attack, in which the
attacker can only query the model and obtain a discrete prediction label.
Existing hard-label attack algorithms tend to initialize adversarial examples
by random substitution and then utilize complex heuristic algorithms to
optimize the adversarial perturbation. These methods require a lot of model
queries and the attack success rate is restricted by adversary initialization.
In this paper, we propose a novel hard-label attack algorithm named LimeAttack,
which leverages a local explainable method to approximate word importance
ranking, and then adopts beam search to find the optimal solution. Extensive
experiments show that LimeAttack achieves the better attacking performance
compared with existing hard-label attack under the same query budget. In
addition, we evaluate the effectiveness of LimeAttack on large language models,
and results indicate that adversarial examples remain a significant threat to
large language models. The adversarial examples crafted by LimeAttack are
highly transferable and effectively improve model robustness in adversarial
training.

Question: What was the specific improvement in BLEU score when using the TreeSwap data augmentation method on the English-to-German law domain dataset?
Summary:
Title: TreeSwap: Data Augmentation for Machine Translation via Dependency
  Subtree Swapping 
Summary: Data augmentation methods for neural machine translation are particularly
useful when limited amount of training data is available, which is often the
case when dealing with low-resource languages. We introduce a novel
augmentation method, which generates new sentences by swapping objects and
subjects across bisentences. This is performed simultaneously based on the
dependency parse trees of the source and target sentences. We name this method
TreeSwap. Our results show that TreeSwap achieves consistent improvements over
baseline models in 4 language pairs in both directions on resource-constrained
datasets. We also explore domain-specific corpora, but find that our method
does not make significant improvements on law, medical and IT data. We report
the scores of similar augmentation methods and find that TreeSwap performs
comparably. We also analyze the generated sentences qualitatively and find that
the augmentation produces a correct translation in most cases. Our code is
available on Github.

Question: What was the specific parameter size of the largest WizardCoder model evaluated on the HumanEval+ benchmark?
Summary:
Title: WizardCoder: Empowering Code Large Language Models with Evol-Instruct 
Summary: Code Large Language Models (Code LLMs), such as StarCoder, have demonstrated
exceptional performance in code-related tasks. However, most existing models
are solely pre-trained on extensive raw code data without instruction
fine-tuning. In this paper, we introduce WizardCoder, which empowers Code LLMs
with complex instruction fine-tuning, by adapting the Evol-Instruct method to
the domain of code. Through comprehensive experiments on four prominent code
generation benchmarks, namely HumanEval, HumanEval+, MBPP, and DS-1000, we
unveil the exceptional capabilities of our model. It surpasses all other
open-source Code LLMs by a substantial margin. Moreover, our model even
outperforms the largest closed LLMs, Anthropic's Claude and Google's Bard, on
HumanEval and HumanEval+. Our code, model weights, and data are public at
https://github.com/nlpxucan/WizardLM
